title: Hank's Night in the Sun

jokes.text: The next time one of you shows up wearing black sneakers instead of black dress shoes, you'll need 'em because I'll chase your delicate little butt right out into the street.
jokes.setup: Artie lecturing staff about proper footwear for television work
jokes.punchline: Escalates from dress code concern to physical chase threat
jokes.explanation: Perfect character moment for Artie - his obsession with protocol escalating to absurd physical threats. The specificity of 'delicate little butt' adds character voice.

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: The next time one of you shows up wearing black sneakers instead of black dress shoes, you'll need 'em because I'll chase your delicate little butt right out into the street.

jokes.text: Because he thinks if you can see the colors, it's easier to pick the flavor.
jokes.setup: Why Larry has to go into the yogurt place in person
jokes.punchline: Absurd logic that frozen yogurt color visibility aids flavor selection
jokes.explanation: Excellent character comedy - captures Larry's neurotic attention to meaningless details with perfect specificity and economy.

dialogue.line: Because he thinks if you can see the colors, it's easier to pick the flavor.

jokes.text: Thank god, because I've seen Larry vomit. It's not something you want to watch over and over again on Hard Copy.
jokes.setup: Relief that Larry's public vomiting had no witnesses
jokes.punchline: Artie's specific knowledge of Larry's unpleasant vomiting style
jokes.explanation: Dark comedy gold - Artie's matter-of-fact delivery about Larry's disgusting bodily functions, with specific media reference adding authenticity.

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: Thank god, because I've seen Larry vomit. It's not something you want to watch over and over again on Hard Copy.

jokes.text: You're right. Flop Howie and Louie, bump Saget from third to fourth, and make sure that Seinfeld knows that he's our first choice.
jokes.setup: Paula questioning the comedian hierarchy
jokes.punchline: Artie's instant strategic recalibration of the desperation order
jokes.explanation: Excellent escalation of the hierarchy joke - Artie's instant strategic thinking shows his manipulative producer instincts.

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: You're right. Flop Howie and Louie, bump Saget from third to fourth, and make sure that Seinfeld knows that he's our first choice.

jokes.text: When he says no, make sure that Richard Lewis thinks that he's our first choice, and so on down the list.
jokes.setup: The comedian calling strategy
jokes.punchline: Artie's systematic deception plan for each comedian's ego
jokes.explanation: Perfect industry satire - captures the systematic ego-stroking manipulation that defines Hollywood relationships with surgical precision.

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: When he says no, make sure that Richard Lewis thinks that he's our first choice, and so on down the list.

jokes.text: Oh, your mind amazes me! I'm just now comfortable with it myself.
jokes.setup: Paula praising Artie's manipulative strategy
jokes.punchline: Artie's casual admission of his own calculating nature
jokes.explanation: Great character moment - Artie's self-aware acknowledgment of his manipulative skills delivered with perfect deadpan timing.

dialogue.speaker: Paula
dialogue.line: Oh, your mind amazes me!

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: I'm just now comfortable with it myself.

jokes.text: 80% unemployment in the acting ranks, you can't find me one name?
jokes.setup: Frustration at inability to book emergency host
jokes.punchline: Industry statistic highlighting the absurdity of the booking failure
jokes.explanation: Solid industry joke with real statistics, though somewhat conventional in execution.

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: 80% unemployment in the acting ranks, you can't find me one name?

jokes.text: Well, there's some chimps at Live at 5 who might be available.
jokes.setup: Artie demanding any available talent
jokes.punchline: Paula's deadpan suggestion of literal animals as hosts
jokes.explanation: Nice absurdist escalation delivered deadpan, though doesn't feel fully character-integrated for Paula.

dialogue.speaker: Paula
dialogue.line: Well, there's some chimps at Live at 5 who might be available.

jokes.text: Paula, are you aware I'm about to open the lid of a box far nastier than Pandora's?
jokes.setup: Artie building dramatic tension about his solution
jokes.punchline: Classical mythology reference for asking Hank to host
jokes.explanation: Excellent setup - Artie's dramatic flair and classical reference perfectly frame the horror of turning to Hank, with great quotable potential.

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: Paula, are you aware I'm about to open the lid of a box far nastier than Pandora's?

jokes.text: I'm not supposed to eat in my show clothes. I know. It's just...I get hungry.
jokes.setup: Hank caught eating in his performance outfit
jokes.punchline: His childlike explanation for breaking wardrobe protocol
jokes.explanation: Perfect Hank character moment - his simpleton logic and inability to follow basic professional protocols.

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: I'm not supposed to eat in my show clothes. I know. It's just...I get hungry.

jokes.text: My psychic told me a man in my life was going to be given a great opportunity. I thought she meant my father was going to get a job, but--but this, this is good, too.
jokes.setup: Hank explaining his psychic's prediction
jokes.punchline: His assumption the prediction was about his unemployed father
jokes.explanation: Great character work - reveals Hank's gullibility, family dysfunction, and self-centeredness in one economical beat.

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: My psychic told me a man in my life was going to be given a great opportunity. I thought she meant my father was going to get a job, but--but this, this is good, too.

jokes.text: I can't move my legs. Whoop. Hip.
jokes.setup: Hank paralyzed by the pressure of hosting
jokes.punchline: Artie's casual physical adjustment fixes Hank's psychosomatic paralysis
jokes.explanation: Nice physical comedy moment showing Hank's psychological fragility and Artie's pragmatic problem-solving.

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: I can't move my legs.

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: Whoop. Hip.

jokes.text: I'm doing some creative visualization, and I'm seeing the audience screaming with laughter. I'm seeing the same thing, Darlene. Thank you for explaining the screaming.
jokes.setup: Hank sharing his visualization of audience laughter
jokes.punchline: Artie's dry clarification that he also sees screaming, but for different reasons
jokes.explanation: Excellent misdirection - Artie's deadpan implication that the audience will scream in horror rather than laughter, perfectly delivered.

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: I'm doing some creative visualization, and I'm seeing the audience screaming with laughter.

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: I'm seeing the same thing, Darlene.

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: Thank you for explaining the screaming.

jokes.text: And, uh, I want to eat some fruit. I don't know why... A pineapple.
jokes.setup: Hank making pre-show requests
jokes.punchline: His random, specific craving for an impractical fruit
jokes.explanation: Perfect Hank logic - random, impractical demands delivered with complete seriousness. Sets up running gag.

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: And, uh, I want to eat some fruit. I don't know why... A pineapple.

jokes.text: Artie, my pal, Shadoe Stevens, he's 2 blocks away. Why don't we call him, have him do the warm-up and the intro? Shadoe is 2 blocks away. Did I say that already?
jokes.setup: Hank suggesting his friend for the show
jokes.punchline: His nervous repetition reveals his scattered mental state
jokes.explanation: Good character work showing Hank's nervousness through repetitive speech patterns, though not particularly memorable.

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: Artie, my pal, Shadoe Stevens, he's 2 blocks away. Why don't we call him, have him do the warm-up and the intro? Shadoe is 2 blocks away. Did I say that already?

jokes.text: It's just such a wonderful, tremendous vote of confidence. I want to thank you. Hank, believe me, I had no choice. I know. Thank you.
jokes.setup: Hank thanking Artie for the hosting opportunity
jokes.punchline: Artie's brutal honesty that Hank was the last resort
jokes.explanation: Perfect cringe comedy - Artie's crushing honesty followed by Hank's pathetic gratitude anyway. Excellent structure and character work.

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: It's just such a wonderful, tremendous vote of confidence. I want to thank you.

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: Hank, believe me, I had no choice.

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: I know. Thank you.

jokes.text: My, god. Larry's sick? Yeah. Food poisoning. Didn't anybody tell you? No!
jokes.setup: Going over monologue cards with Hank
jokes.punchline: Hank realizes he never processed why he's hosting
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Hank's self-absorption - so focused on his opportunity he missed the basic premise.

dialogue.speaker: Sid
dialogue.line: My, god. Larry's sick?

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: Yeah. Food poisoning.

dialogue.speaker: Sid
dialogue.line: Didn't anybody tell you?

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: No!

jokes.text: I guess Artie did, but I was so nervous, and the shock and the excitement, uh...
jokes.setup: Hank explaining why he didn't process Larry being sick
jokes.punchline: His admission of complete self-absorption during the crisis
jokes.explanation: Solid character work but somewhat expected - Hank's selfishness is well-established by this point.

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: I guess Artie did, but I was so nervous, and the shock and the excitement, uh...

jokes.text: 'Even though Larry couldn't be here tonight, I know he's comfortable. I have an american car. The trunk is quite spacious.' Flip it. No, that's it, Hank. What? That's the joke.
jokes.setup: Hank reading monologue joke about Larry's comfort
jokes.punchline: Hank doesn't understand the kidnapping implication in his own monologue
jokes.explanation: Great meta-comedy - Hank missing the dark humor in his own material reveals both his innocence and comedic ineptitude.

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: Even though Larry couldn't be here tonight, I know he's comfortable. I have an american car. The trunk is quite spacious.

dialogue.speaker: Sid
dialogue.line: Flip it. No, that's it, Hank.

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: What?

dialogue.speaker: Sid
dialogue.line: That's the joke.

jokes.text: 'Even though Larry couldn't be here tonight, I know he's comfortable. I have an american car, and I put him in the trunk, and I kidnapped him.'
jokes.setup: Hank trying to 'fix' the monologue joke
jokes.punchline: His literal explanation destroys all subtlety and humor
jokes.explanation: Brilliant comedy writing - Hank's literal-minded 'improvement' perfectly demonstrates why he shouldn't be hosting. Meta-joke about joke construction.

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: Even though Larry couldn't be here tonight, I know he's comfortable. I have an american car, and I put him in the trunk, and I kidnapped him.

jokes.text: Actually, we're off the list--Hank. [Retching] Larry? Artie, it's Beverly. Larry's vomiting again. Can we get back to you?
jokes.setup: Artie tells Larry that Hank is hosting
jokes.punchline: Larry immediately starts vomiting again upon hearing the news
jokes.explanation: Perfect physical comedy - Larry's visceral reaction to Hank hosting is both immediate and extreme. Excellent structure and economy.

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: Actually, we're off the list--Hank.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: [Retching]

dialogue.speaker: Beverly
dialogue.line: Larry? Artie, it's Beverly. Larry's vomiting again. Can we get back to you?

jokes.text: I'm Phil, the best writer. Thanks for doing the show. No problem. There's been a little change.
jokes.setup: Phil introducing himself to guest George Wendt
jokes.punchline: His casual self-aggrandizing introduction
jokes.explanation: Solid character moment showing writer's ego, though fairly standard industry satire.

dialogue.speaker: Phil
dialogue.line: I'm Phil, the best writer. Thanks for doing the show.

dialogue.speaker: George Wendt
dialogue.line: No problem. There's been a little change.

jokes.text: Who's gonna host? Uh...Hank. Hank? Well, that's cool. Oh, my god. Oh, my god.
jokes.setup: George asking who will host instead of Larry
jokes.punchline: Phil's increasing panic at George's polite acceptance
jokes.explanation: Good escalation comedy - Phil's horror at George's politeness about the hosting disaster.

dialogue.speaker: George Wendt
dialogue.line: Who's gonna host? Uh...Hank.

dialogue.speaker: Phil
dialogue.line: Hank?

dialogue.speaker: George Wendt
dialogue.line: Well, that's cool.

dialogue.speaker: Phil
dialogue.line: Oh, my god. Oh, my god.

jokes.text: I did Conan's first show, so... You have to remember. Yes, I do, and I forgive you.
jokes.setup: George comparing Hank's debut to Conan's rocky start
jokes.punchline: Phil's mock-forgiveness for George's career misstep
jokes.explanation: Sharp industry joke with good deadpan delivery - references real TV history with perfect comedic timing.

dialogue.speaker: George Wendt
dialogue.line: I did Conan's first show, so... You have to remember.

dialogue.speaker: Phil
dialogue.line: Yes, I do, and I forgive you.

jokes.text: Kings...Kingsley! Kings-leyyyy! Kind of a flaired-out riff. No. B-flat. Kings-ley! Kings-leyyyyy!
jokes.setup: Shadoe coaching Hank on his introduction style
jokes.punchline: Elaborate musical direction for a simple name announcement
jokes.explanation: Good character work for both - Shadoe's over-the-top professionalism meets Hank's desperate need for validation.

dialogue.speaker: Shadoe Stevens
dialogue.line: Kings...Kingsley! Kings-leyyyy! Kind of a flaired-out riff. No. B-flat. Kings-ley! Kings-leyyyyy!

jokes.text: I'm just trying to pick out the right tie for the show. What about this one? You think that one? Oh, definitely. Ok. That's the one. You're the boss.
jokes.setup: Hank seeking wardrobe approval from Artie
jokes.punchline: His desperate need for validation on trivial decisions
jokes.explanation: Solid character work showing Hank's insecurity, though fairly predictable for his character.

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: I'm just trying to pick out the right tie for the show. What about this one? You think that one?

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: Oh, definitely.

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: Ok. That's the one. You're the boss.

jokes.text: And, uh, you know, I ordered some pineapple. Have you seen my pineapple?
jokes.setup: Hank continuing his pre-show preparations
jokes.punchline: His persistent focus on the impractical pineapple request
jokes.explanation: Effective running gag reinforcing Hank's scattered priorities and odd fixations.

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: And, uh, you know, I ordered some pineapple. Have you seen my pineapple?

jokes.text: Don't make me do this. Ok? I just can't do this. I'm not... I'm not up to this. Don't make me.
jokes.setup: Hank's confidence completely crumbling before showtime
jokes.punchline: His childlike pleading to be released from the opportunity he wanted
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Hank's psychological fragility when faced with actual responsibility.

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: Don't make me do this. Ok? I just can't do this. I'm not... I'm not up to this. Don't make me.

jokes.text: What if I suck? Never! Yeah, what if I suck? After all this time, I finally get my chance, and what if I just suck?
jokes.setup: Hank's pre-show panic about his performance
jokes.punchline: His repetitive existential dread about failure
jokes.explanation: Effective character work showing the universal fear of failure, made specific through Hank's neurotic repetition.

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: What if I suck?

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: Never!

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: Yeah, what if I suck? After all this time, I finally get my chance, and what if I just suck?

jokes.text: What are you drinking? Red zinger and gin.
jokes.setup: Hank noticing Paula's drink during his panic
jokes.punchline: Paula's casual admission of her bizarre alcohol combination
jokes.explanation: Nice specific detail that reveals character - Paula's stress-drinking with an absurd tea-gin combination.

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: What are you drinking?

dialogue.speaker: Paula
dialogue.line: Red zinger and gin.

jokes.text: I been in this business, man and boy, almost 40 years. I know things, Hank. Let me tell you one of the things I know. You do not suck!
jokes.setup: Artie's dramatic buildup to share his industry wisdom
jokes.punchline: His profound insight is simply that Hank doesn't suck
jokes.explanation: Perfect Artie moment - his gruff industry veteran persona delivering surprisingly simple encouragement with perfect timing.

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: I been in this business, man and boy, almost 40 years. I know things, Hank. Let me tell you one of the things I know. You do not suck!

jokes.text: Now, before we goes... Before we goes on...
jokes.setup: Hank's opening hosting attempt
jokes.punchline: His immediate grammatical error and self-correction on live TV
jokes.explanation: Good cringe moment showing Hank's nervousness affecting his basic speech patterns on his big moment.

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: Now, before we goes... Before we goes on...

jokes.text: Even though Larry wouldn't be... couldn't be here tonight, I know he's comfortable. I have an american car, and the trunk is quite spacious.
jokes.setup: Hank delivering the kidnapping monologue joke
jokes.punchline: The joke works despite his earlier misunderstanding of it
jokes.explanation: Effective callback to earlier scene - the joke works in performance despite Hank's earlier confusion about it.

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: Even though Larry wouldn't be... couldn't be here tonight, I know he's comfortable. I have an american car, and the trunk is quite spacious.

jokes.text: Tennis star Jennifer Capriati is in the drug rehab. I mean, is this really a surprise? I seem to remember people all the time saying she preferred playing on grass.
jokes.setup: Celebrity's drug rehab news
jokes.punchline: Tennis surface pun connecting 'grass' court to marijuana
jokes.explanation: Decent wordplay joke but feels like generic late-night material rather than character-specific humor for Hank.

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: Tennis star Jennifer Capriati is in the drug rehab. I mean, is this really a surprise? I seem to remember people all the time saying she preferred playing on grass.

jokes.text: He just got his first big laugh. Good for Hank. You don't understand. This is gonna go right to his head.
jokes.setup: Report on Hank's successful joke delivery
jokes.punchline: Artie's immediate dread about Hank's ego inflation
jokes.explanation: Great character moment showing Artie's knowledge of Hank's psychology - success will make him insufferable.

dialogue.speaker: Beverly
dialogue.line: He just got his first big laugh. Good for Hank.

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: You don't understand. This is gonna go right to his head.

jokes.text: You know, my father always told me to tell the truth, so if it isn't obvious, I'm really terrified. So you're probably not going to see a really great show tonight.
jokes.setup: Hank's honest confession to the audience
jokes.punchline: His self-deprecating admission sets realistic expectations
jokes.explanation: Effective character moment - Hank's honesty and vulnerability create genuine audience connection, showing his hosting instincts.

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: You know, my father always told me to tell the truth, so if it isn't obvious, I'm really terrified. So you're probably not going to see a really great show tonight.

jokes.text: Go, Hank, go! [Chanting] Hank! Hank! Hank!
jokes.setup: Audience responding to Hank's vulnerability
jokes.punchline: Spontaneous chanting support for the underdog host
jokes.explanation: Good escalation showing how Hank's honesty wins over the audience, creating momentum for his success.

dialogue.speaker: Audience
dialogue.line: Go, Hank, go! [Chanting] Hank! Hank! Hank!

jokes.text: Hank, cards are a crutch. They're a crutch. There goes-- Thank you and good night. Hank, you don't need the cards.
jokes.setup: George trying to help Hank by taking away his cue cards
jokes.punchline: The cards literally scatter everywhere, creating chaos
jokes.explanation: Good physical comedy moment - George's helpful gesture backfires spectacularly, creating live TV chaos.

dialogue.speaker: George Wendt
dialogue.line: Hank, cards are a crutch. They're a crutch. There goes-- Thank you and good night. Hank, you don't need the cards.

jokes.text: I have to be honest. I thought we were going to have to warn people to watch this show through a pin hole and a piece of cardboard.
jokes.setup: Artie's post-show assessment of Hank's performance
jokes.punchline: Eclipse viewing metaphor for how unwatchable he expected it to be
jokes.explanation: Brilliant metaphor - comparing watching Hank host to staring at a solar eclipse captures both the potential damage and morbid fascination perfectly.

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: I have to be honest. I thought we were going to have to warn people to watch this show through a pin hole and a piece of cardboard.

jokes.text: I have to be honest. I thought we were going to have to warn people to watch this show through a pin hole and a piece of cardboard.
jokes.setup: Artie's post-show assessment of Hank's performance
jokes.punchline: Eclipse safety metaphor for how unwatchable he expected it to be
jokes.explanation: Exceptional metaphor - comparing Hank's hosting to looking at a solar eclipse. Creative, specific, and perfectly Artie.

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: I have to be honest. I thought we were going to have to warn people to watch this show through a pin hole and a piece of cardboard.

jokes.text: Now I know why Larry is so puffed up.
jokes.setup: Hank reflecting on his hosting experience
jokes.punchline: His immediate understanding of how success inflates ego
jokes.explanation: Good character insight - Hank immediately grasps how hosting success feeds ego, foreshadowing his transformation.

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: Now I know why Larry is so puffed up.

jokes.text: Well, Artie, he's over that food poisoning, but last night, he ran into the bathroom, and he slipped and fell and hit his head on the john.
jokes.setup: Update on Larry's recovery from illness
jokes.punchline: His recovery is immediately complicated by a bathroom injury
jokes.explanation: Solid escalation of Larry's physical comedy misfortunes, though somewhat predictable slapstick.

dialogue.speaker: Beverly
dialogue.line: Well, Artie, he's over that food poisoning, but last night, he ran into the bathroom, and he slipped and fell and hit his head on the john.

jokes.text: It's serious enough to get those pain killers he's been after. Yay!
jokes.setup: Larry's head injury assessment
jokes.punchline: Beverly's cheerful acknowledgment of Larry's drug-seeking behavior
jokes.explanation: Dark humor revealing both Larry's pill-seeking and Beverly's casual enabling of his behavior with cheerful delivery.

dialogue.speaker: Beverly
dialogue.line: It's serious enough to get those pain killers he's been after. Yay!

jokes.text: You sound a little surprised I did so well. Oh? I'm glad I'm not playing poker anytime soon.
jokes.setup: Hank detecting Artie's surprise at his success
jokes.punchline: Artie's admission that his expectations were transparently low
jokes.explanation: Sharp character moment - Artie's poker reference acknowledges his inability to hide his low expectations for Hank.

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: You sound a little surprised I did so well.

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: Oh? I'm glad I'm not playing poker anytime soon.

jokes.text: No. No, I book talent. I don't get coffee. Then could you book David Copperfield on the show, and when he gets here, have him pull a cup of coffee out of his ass?
jokes.setup: Paula refusing to get Hank coffee based on job description
jokes.punchline: Hank's elaborate magical solution to his coffee problem
jokes.explanation: Brilliant escalation - Hank's absurd booking request perfectly captures his new sense of entitlement and creative problem-solving.

dialogue.speaker: Paula
dialogue.line: No. No, I book talent. I don't get coffee.

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: Then could you book David Copperfield on the show, and when he gets here, have him pull a cup of coffee out of his ass?

jokes.text: Well, correct me if I'm wrong, but the procedure is the guest host gets to use Larry's office, right? Yes. The guest host, which you are not.
jokes.setup: Hank claiming guest host privileges for Larry's office
jokes.punchline: Beverly's brutal clarification of his actual status
jokes.explanation: Perfect power struggle moment - Beverly's precise deflation of Hank's inflated sense of status and entitlement.

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: Well, correct me if I'm wrong, but the procedure is the guest host gets to use Larry's office, right?

dialogue.speaker: Beverly
dialogue.line: Yes. The guest host, which you are not.

jokes.text: Because if I do, you know he'll give me the go-ahead to kick your balls right out through the top of your head! Oh, language!
jokes.setup: Beverly threatening to call Larry about Hank's behavior
jokes.punchline: Her graphic threat followed by Hank's prudish reaction
jokes.explanation: Excellent character contrast - Beverly's colorful violence meets Hank's shocked propriety, perfectly executed escalation.

dialogue.speaker: Beverly
dialogue.line: Because if I do, you know he'll give me the go-ahead to kick your balls right out through the top of your head!

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: Oh, language!

jokes.text: Hey. Hey, I saved your asses last night. This is the--the fucking gratitude I get?
jokes.setup: Hank's outrage at being replaced after one successful show
jokes.punchline: His profane demand for gratitude and recognition
jokes.explanation: Good character development - shows how quickly success has transformed Hank into an entitled performer.

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: Hey. Hey, I saved your asses last night. This is the--the fucking gratitude I get?

jokes.text: 'Kingsley proved himself completely maladroit in the host's chair.' Hank, 'adroit' means 'able.' 'Mal' means 'bad' or 'not.' Put 'em together. Maladroit--not able. capisce?
jokes.setup: Reading negative review that Hank doesn't understand
jokes.punchline: Artie's condescending vocabulary lesson about the insult
jokes.explanation: Excellent deflation moment - Artie's pedagogical explanation of how badly Hank was insulted, delivered with perfect condescension.

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: 'Kingsley proved himself completely maladroit in the host's chair.' Hank, 'adroit' means 'able.' 'Mal' means 'bad' or 'not.' Put 'em together. Maladroit--not able. capisce?

jokes.text: Well, it's funny because last week, Larry and Arthur were talking, and one of them said that they thought the whole neurotic Jewish person thing that you do is, uh-- What'd he say? Predictably, uh, banal.
jokes.setup: Hank's fake-casual conversation with Richard Lewis
jokes.punchline: His delivery of devastating criticism disguised as gossip
jokes.explanation: Brilliant character sabotage - Hank's passive-aggressive delivery of harsh criticism perfectly captures his newfound manipulative streak.

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: Well, it's funny because last week, Larry and Arthur were talking, and one of them said that they thought the whole neurotic Jewish person thing that you do is, uh-- What'd he say? Predictably, uh, banal.

jokes.text: The whole neurotic Jewish person thing that you do is, uh-- What'd he say? Predictably, uh, banal.
jokes.setup: Hank trying to sabotage Richard Lewis
jokes.punchline: Fabricating harsh criticism of Lewis's comedy style
jokes.explanation: Perfect sabotage attempt - Hank attacking Lewis's core comedic identity with fake insider knowledge.

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: The whole neurotic Jewish person thing that you do is, uh-- What'd he say? Predictably, uh, banal.

jokes.text: I'm sure Hank got on the phone with him. That crazy son of a bitch. I don't think Richard's that crazy. I think that's just an act. I meant Hank.
jokes.setup: Discussion of Richard Lewis dropping out
jokes.punchline: Larry's clarification that Hank is the actual crazy one
jokes.explanation: Nice misdirection - Larry's clarification that Hank, not Richard Lewis, is the genuinely unstable one.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: I'm sure Hank got on the phone with him. That crazy son of a bitch. I don't think Richard's that crazy. I think that's just an act. I meant Hank.

jokes.text: Last night he was a crying mess... Now he's the ass of the western world.
jokes.setup: Artie describing Hank's transformation
jokes.punchline: Geographic hyperbole for Hank's newfound obnoxiousness
jokes.explanation: Great character assessment - Artie's colorful description of Hank's rapid transformation from pathetic to insufferable.

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: Last night he was a crying mess... Now he's the ass of the western world.

jokes.text: 'I think socks had something to do with that decision.' It's not funny. I beg your pardon? What word do you have trouble with? You understand 'not'? That's comfortable for you? And 'funny.' You've heard of that?
jokes.setup: Writer questioning Hank's rejection of a joke
jokes.punchline: Hank's condescending vocabulary lesson about basic concepts
jokes.explanation: Perfect character corruption - Hank's transformation into an abusive boss, delivered with escalating condescension.

dialogue.speaker: Writer
dialogue.line: I think socks had something to do with that decision.

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: It's not funny. I beg your pardon? What word do you have trouble with? You understand 'not'? That's comfortable for you? And 'funny.' You've heard of that?

jokes.text: What? What?! You've changed, Hank. There's a darkness around you. Good, fine. There's a darkness.
jokes.setup: Darlene observing Hank's personality change
jokes.punchline: Hank's casual acceptance of his moral transformation
jokes.explanation: Excellent character moment - Hank's matter-of-fact acceptance of his moral corruption, perfectly delivered.

dialogue.speaker: Darlene
dialogue.line: What? What?! You've changed, Hank. There's a darkness around you.

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: Good, fine. There's a darkness.

jokes.text: I prayed. I mean, I actually got down on my knees, and I prayed that Larry would stay sick so I could host the show tonight. I mean, I wished this man unwell.
jokes.setup: Hank's confession about his prayers
jokes.punchline: His horrifying admission of wishing harm to his colleague
jokes.explanation: Dark character revelation - Hank's honest confession of his malicious prayers shows the depth of his corruption.

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: I prayed. I mean, I actually got down on my knees, and I prayed that Larry would stay sick so I could host the show tonight. I mean, I wished this man unwell.

jokes.text: See, that's interesting because I told our head writer that that was, you know, a little off, but he's such a hopeless retard that...
jokes.setup: Joke gets weak response
jokes.punchline: Hank using slur to blame the writer on live TV
jokes.explanation: Perfect career-ending moment - Hank's ego making him attack his own staff with slurs on air. Character-driven disaster.

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: See, that's interesting because I told our head writer that that was, you know, a little off, but he's such a hopeless retard that...

jokes.text: You see, it's not Larry who is sick. It's me. Yes, it's me. I am very sick. I am a sicko. I'm so fucking sick. But I'm finally where I belong.
jokes.setup: Hank's realization about who's really sick
jokes.punchline: His embrace of his moral sickness as his true nature
jokes.explanation: Brilliant character climax - Hank's full acceptance of his corruption with the twisted logic that this is his authentic self. Powerful writing.

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: You see, it's not Larry who is sick. It's me. Yes, it's me. I am very sick. I am a sicko. I'm so fucking sick. But I'm finally where I belong.

jokes.text: I'm sure socks had something to do with that decision. See, that's interesting because I told our head writer that that was, you know, a little off, but he's such a hopeless retard that...
jokes.setup: Hank delivering the socks joke he previously rejected
jokes.punchline: His on-air attack on the writer using offensive language
jokes.explanation: Effective character corruption moment - Hank's public humiliation of staff shows his complete transformation into a monster.

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: I'm sure socks had something to do with that decision. See, that's interesting because I told our head writer that that was, you know, a little off, but he's such a hopeless retard that...

jokes.text: You suck! Take a seat.
jokes.setup: Audience member's hostile reaction to Hank's performance
jokes.punchline: Hank's casual dismissal of the heckler
jokes.explanation: Good beat showing Hank's newfound arrogance in handling criticism, though fairly straightforward.

dialogue.speaker: Audience member
dialogue.line: You suck!

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: Take a seat.

jokes.text: Oh, no, Hank. It really got away. It's down in Bolivia by now.
jokes.setup: Hank saying his show 'got away from him'
jokes.punchline: Artie's geographic metaphor for how far it went wrong
jokes.explanation: Brilliant metaphor - Artie's geographic escalation of how badly things went. Creative and perfectly delivered.

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: Oh, no, Hank. It really got away. It's down in Bolivia by now.

jokes.text: You should send 3 baskets to Lionel Richie, one for each time you called him Little Richard.
jokes.setup: Artie listing Hank's offensive mistakes
jokes.punchline: Multiple instances of the same celebrity confusion
jokes.explanation: Good escalation joke showing the pattern of Hank's celebrity name confusion with specific quantification.

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: You should send 3 baskets to Lionel Richie, one for each time you called him Little Richard.

jokes.text: When you're vulnerable and humble, people like you. When you act like an asshole, people tend to think of you as an asshole.
jokes.setup: Artie's lesson about audience psychology
jokes.punchline: His brutally simple explanation of cause and effect
jokes.explanation: Perfect Artie wisdom - his matter-of-fact explanation of human nature delivered with characteristic bluntness.

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: When you're vulnerable and humble, people like you. When you act like an asshole, people tend to think of you as an asshole.

jokes.text: 1--when you're sick that close to air, we'll cancel. 2--Hank's place is on the couch where he does his best work. And 3--always let Beverly go into that yogurt shop alone.
jokes.setup: Artie's lessons learned from the crisis
jokes.punchline: The absurd practicality of the yogurt shop lesson
jokes.explanation: Nice callback structure with escalating absurdity - from professional to personal to completely mundane lessons.

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: when you're sick that close to air, we'll cancel. Hank's place is on the couch where he does his best work. And always let Beverly go into that yogurt shop alone.

jokes.text: I'll admit, I may have mishandled this affair, but everything turned out for the best.
jokes.setup: Artie reflecting on the disaster
jokes.punchline: Claiming the catastrophe worked out well
jokes.explanation: Perfect character denial - Artie's refusal to admit complete failure. The understatement is perfect.

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: I'll admit, I may have mishandled this affair, but everything turned out for the best.

jokes.text: Hey, Artie? What's happenin'?
jokes.setup: Artie's confident assessment that everything worked out
jokes.punchline: Larry's immediate return with casual greeting, showing nothing has changed
jokes.explanation: Perfect ending beat - Larry's casual return immediately after Artie's speech about lessons learned, showing the cyclical nature of their world.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: Hey, Artie? What's happenin'?

tmdb_overview: Hank gets his shot at the big chair when Larry gets food poisoning just before a taping.
